text: Giulio Tononi's Integrated Information Theory proposes a radical answer to the hard problem: consciousness is identical to integrated information, measured by the quantity phi. A system is conscious to the degree that its parts generate more information together than they do separately. This definition does not privilege biology — a sufficiently complex network of any material could, in principle, be conscious. But it also implies counterintuitive results: a photodiode has a vanishingly small but nonzero phi, while certain simple feedforward neural networks, despite their processing power, may have near-zero consciousness. For Tononi, the structure of integration, not the substrate, is what gives rise to experience.
question: According to Tononi's Integrated Information Theory, what determines whether a system is conscious?

options.text: Whether the system is made of biological neurons capable of firing action potentials
The degree to which its parts generate more information together than separately, measured as phi
The total number of distinct states the system can represent in working memory
Whether the system can pass a behavioral test for self-awareness
